original_abstract: Abstract The three-dimensional structure of carbohelicenes has fascinated generations of molecular chemists and has been exploited in a wide range of applications. Their strong circularly polarized luminescence has attracted considerable attention in recent years due to promising applications in new optical materials. Although the enantioselective synthesis of fused carbo- and heterohelicenes has been achieved, a direct catalytic enantioselective method allowing the synthesis of lower, non-fused carbo[ n ]helicenes ( n = 4–6) is still lacking. We report here that Pd-catalysed enantioselective C–H arylation in the presence of a unique bifunctional phosphine-carboxylate ligand provides a simple and general access to these lower carbo[ n ]helicenes. Computational mechanistic studies indicate that both the C–H activation and reductive elimination steps contribute to the overall enantioselectivity. The observed enantio-induction seems to arise from a combination of non-covalent interactions and steric repulsion between the substrate and ligand during the two key reductive elimination steps. The photophysical and chiroptical properties of the synthesized scalemic [ n ]helicenes have been systematically studied.
humanized_abstract: The ever-present challenge of synthesizing helical structures such as carbohelicenes in the solid state and their numerous applications have fascinated generations of chemists. The strong CPL emission that can be obtained from these molecules has triggered a renewed interest in these types of materials for potential applications in materials science. The enantioselective synthesis of the fused carbon/hetero-helicenes was achieved several years ago. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is not yet any reported catalytic enantioselective synthesis of lower non-fused carbo[ n ]helicenes ( n = 4–6). In this article we report on the Pd catalysed enantioselective C–H arylation using a bifunctional bifurcate P-OC ligand and show that this method gives access to any of the lower carbo[ n ]helicenes in good yields and enantioselectivity. Modelling studies indicate that the enantioselectivity is the result of a balance between activation energy for the C–H activation and reductive elimination steps. The enantiodetermination of the [ n ]helicene comes from the competition between non-covalent interactions between the substrate and the ligand as well as from steric repulsions during the two reductive elimination steps of the catalytic cycle. Their photophysical and chiroptical properties have been examined in detail over a wide range of concentrations to quantify their potential application to luminescent materials with circularly polarised light emission.
